does: This paper introduces CrossGuard, a defense mechanism for multimodal large language models that protects against both explicit and implicit jailbreak attacks where malicious intent spans across text and image modalities. The authors develop ImpForge, an automated red-teaming pipeline using reinforcement learning with specialized reward modules to generate diverse implicit attack samples across 14 domains, addressing the shortage of high-quality training data for this threat category. CrossGuard uses intent-aware safeguarding to detect and mitigate attacks where benign content in one modality combines with content in another modality to express unsafe goals. The approach is evaluated on safe and unsafe benchmarks, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing defenses including advanced MLLMs and current guardrail systems.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature documenting jailbreak vulnerabilities in large language models and their multimodal variants. It extends prior work on explicit adversarial attacks and robustness testing by identifying and formalizing the more subtle threat of implicit cross-modal attacks, where malicious intent emerges from joint reasoning across modalities. The paper connects to recent red-teaming efforts that use reinforcement learning and automated adversarial generation to uncover model vulnerabilities, while also drawing on intent detection and safety-aligned response frameworks from the alignment research community. The use of RL-based reward shaping for generating diverse attack samples extends techniques previously applied to single-modality jailbreaking.
enables: This work opens new research directions in multimodal safety by establishing implicit cross-modal attacks as a critical evaluation frontier for MLLMs. The ImpForge pipeline provides a reusable framework for generating domain-diverse implicit attacks, enabling broader systematic evaluation of multimodal safety across different model architectures and training approaches. CrossGuard's intent-aware defense architecture suggests pathways toward more sophisticated alignment methods that reason about intention rather than surface-level content, potentially applicable to other safety-critical multimodal systems. The comprehensive benchmark of implicit attacks and defenses could motivate future work on fundamental architectural improvements to MLLMs that inherently resist joint-modal reasoning attacks, as well as better training methodologies that maintain capability while ensuring safety.
